title: Wearable data link urban green space to physical activity
data_sources: NIH All of Us Research Program (Fitbit wearable data)
Trust for Public Land ParkServe database
US Census Bureau American Community Survey 5-year estimates (2014-2018)
NOAA Climate Data Record (temperature and precipitation)

data_scope.spatial_unit: three-digit zipcode; CBG for park accessibility

core_conclusion: A 10% increase in park accessibility is associated with 107 additional daily steps (a 100% increase with 1,067 additional steps) among 7,013 Fitbit users across 53 US cities, whereas general greenness (NDVI) shows no significant step-count association. The effect is strongest for elderly, Black, and Latino residents and in western and southern US cities, consistent with an equigenic benefit of accessible urban parks.
why_it_matters: First nationwide longitudinal wearable study separating park accessibility from general greenness; quantifies a clinically meaningful step-count signal; exposes demographic and geographic heterogeneity directing park investment; supports the equigenic mechanism that parks disproportionately benefit under-served populations.
why_nature: Nationwide wearable-device data as population-level behavioural surveillance; mechanism plus policy translation; health-disparities contribution for elderly, Black, and Latino residents; novel triangulation of NIH cohort data with CBG-level park accessibility and three-level HLM.
added_value_to_my_research: GPS activity-space extension of the paper's UGCoP limitation -> individual-level 2SFCA catchment areas grounded in behaviour
MGWR/GWR applied to city-level park-step slopes -> continuous spatial surface of park-activity responsiveness for adaptive bandwidth calibration
Paper's equigenic finding converted to multi-objective park siting optimization -> efficiency-vs-equity Pareto front for public green infrastructure
Multi-source fusion (wearable + mobile + remote sensing + street-view) -> GeoAI health observatory benchmarking geospatial foundation models
Paper's empirically validated Gaussian decay (d0=1 km) as behaviorally grounded prior for R2SFCA healthcare accessibility models

related.title: Countrywide natural experiment links built environment to physical activity
related.relation: nationwide objective built-environment to physical activity linkage — closest methodological parallel and scale comparator

related.title: Unequal access to social, environmental and health amenities in US urban parks
related.relation: US urban park accessibility equity — directly examines unequal park service distribution, the supply-side counterpart to this paper's demand-side step-count analysis

related.title: Small accessible urban parks mitigate heat-related mortality
related.relation: accessible parks and health outcomes — parallel finding on park configuration and mortality risk in Nature Health

related.title: Impact of daylight saving time on physical activity patterns
related.relation: also uses NIH All of Us Fitbit longitudinal data to study environmental effects on physical activity — same data infrastructure

related.title: Well-being from nature exposure depends on socio-environmental contexts in Paraguay
related.relation: socio-environmental context moderating nature-health associations — extends equigenic debate to Global South settings

related.title: Understanding the potential of urban parks to promote well-being
related.relation: park design typology and well-being mechanisms — complements the step-count behavioural signal with park feature classification

figures.caption: Spatial distribution of participants and average daily step counts across 53 US metropolitan areas (circle size = participant count; colour = mean daily steps)
Park accessibility at the CBG level for eight metropolitan areas, illustrating within-city variation and divergence from NDVI greenness
Pearson correlation matrix of daily steps, park accessibility, NDVI, walkability, temperature, precipitation, AQI, and population density across three-digit zipcode areas
City-level park-step slope deviations by US geographic region, and correlation of park-step slopes with average temperature (r = 0.61, P = 0.0002)
Park-step slopes vs. baseline daily steps, average age, and proportion of White residents across 53 metropolitan areas — evidence for equigenic park effects
